title: Exploring Streamflow Across the Colorado River Basin
text:
if (!requireNamespace("pacman", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("pacman")

pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse,
  dataRetrieval,
  zoo,
  patchwork,
  flextable,
  lubridate,
  broom
)





gauges <- tribble( 
  ~site_no,    ~name,                            ~state,
  "09380000",   "Colorado R at Lees Ferry",      "AZ",
  "09163500",   "Colorado R near CO-UT border",  "CO",
  "09085000",   "Colorado R at Glenwood Springs","CO",
  "09306500",   "White R near Watson",           "UT",
  "09379500",   "San Juan R near Bluff",         "UT",
  "09402500",   "Little Colorado R near Cameron","AZ",
  "09423000",   "Colorado R below Hoover Dam",   "NV"
  
  
)

raw <- readNWISdv(
  siteNumbers = gauges$site_no,
  parameterCd= "00060",
  startDate = "1980-01-01",
  endDate = "2023-12-31"
) |>
  renameNWISColumns() |>
  left_join(gauges, by = "site_no") |>
  select(site_no, name, state, Date, Flow)

date_flows <- df %>% filter(Date == my.date) 

table1_data <- date_flows %>%
  slice_max(order_by = Flow, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  transmute(
    'Site Number' = site_no,

    'State' = state,
    'Date' = Date,
    'Discharge (cfs)' = round(Flow, 1)

    'Date' = "Date",
    'Discharge (cfs)' = "Discharge (cfs)"
  ) %>%
  set_caption("Table 1. The 5 gauges with the highest discharge on the selected date") %>%
  autofit()

Add in each gauge’s drainage area to compare drainage.

  select(
    site_no,
    drain_area_va,   # drainage area in square miles
    huc_cd,          # 8-digit HUC watershed code
    dec_lat_va,      # latitude
    dec_long_va      # longitude
  )

Compare runoff intensity across sites within different drainage areas by normalize by expressing flow per unit area: cfs per square mile.

Create a normalized flow column

basin_flow <- basin_flow %>%
  mutate(flow_per_sqmi = Flow / drain_area_va)

Filter to date and create a ranking table

flow_rank <- basin_flow %>%
  filter(Date == my.date) %>%
  mutate(
    raw_rank = min_rank(desc(Flow)),
    normalized_rank = min_rank(desc(flow_per_sqmi))
  ) %>%
  arrange(raw_rank) %>% 
   transmute(
    'Gauge Name' = name,
    'Raw Discharge (cfs)' = round(Flow, 1),
    'Drainage Area (mi²)' = round(drain_area_va, 1),
    'Normalized Flow (cfs/mi²)' = round(flow_per_sqmi, 3),
    'Raw Rank' = raw_rank,
    'Normalized Rank' = normalized_rank
  )

flow_rank_ft <- flextable(flow_rank) %>%
  set_caption("Raw and normalized discharge rankings for gauges on the selected dates") %>%
  autofit()

flow_rank_ft

Gauge NameRaw Discharge (cfs)Drainage Area (mi²)Normalized Flow (cfs/mi²)Raw RankNormalized RankLittle Colorado R near Cameron6,950141,6000.04915Colorado R at Lees Ferry6,570111,8000.05924Colorado R near CO-UT border3,47017,8490.19432San Juan R near Bluff68023,0000.03046Colorado R at Glenwood Springs5331,4530.36751White R near Watson3294,0200.08263Colorado R below Hoover Dam173,300


The table above shows that raw and normalized rankings differ substantially. The most dramatic change is for Colorado River at Glenwood Springs, which changes from 5th in raw discharge to 1st when normalized, indicating a much higher flow per unit area despite a smaller total flow.
Per-area normalization matters in the Colorado Basin because watersheds vary in size- large rivers naturally carry more total water, but smaller basins can be much more hydrologically “intense” when flow is scaled by drainage area, making comparisons more meaningful.
Raw discharge would be used for analyses such as water supply where total volume matters, while normalized discharge is better for comparing runoff efficiency across basins of different sizes.






basin_flow <- basin_flow %>%
  arrange(site_no, Date) %>%
  group_by(site_no) %>%
  mutate(
    flow_7day = rollmean(Flow, k = 7, fill = NA, align = "right")
  ) %>%
  ungroup()





#Get historical reference
hist_ref <- basin_flow %>%
  filter(Date >= as.Date("1980-01-01"),
         Date <= as.Date("2010-12-31"))

#Compute 10th percentile threshhold
lowflow_thresh <- hist_ref %>%
  group_by(site_no) %>%
  summarize(
    q10_thresh = quantile(flow_7day, probs = 0.10, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

lees_ferry <- basin_flow %>%
  filter(site_no == "09380000",
         Date >= as.Date("2000-01-01"),
         Date <= as.Date("2023-12-31"))


ggplot(lees_ferry, aes(x = Date, y = flow_7day)) +
  geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(
      ymin = 0,
      ymax = ifelse(below_threshold, flow_7day, NA)
    ),
    fill = "red",
    alpha = 0.35
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    aes(yintercept = q10_thresh),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "blue",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Lees Ferry 7-Day Rolling Mean Discharge (2000–2023)",
    subtitle = "Red indicates periods below the 10th percentile threshold",
    x = "Date",
    y = "7-Day Rolling Mean Discharge (cfs)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

(`geom_ribbon()`).













lees_yearly <- basin_flow %>%
  filter(site_no == "09380000",
         Date >= as.Date("2000-01-01"),
         Date <= as.Date("2023-12-31")) %>%
  mutate(period = case_when(
    Date >= as.Date("2000-01-01") & Date <= as.Date("2010-12-31") ~ "2000-2010",
    Date >= as.Date("2011-01-01") & Date <= as.Date("2023-12-31") ~ "2011-2023"
  ),
  year = lubridate::year(Date)) %>%
  group_by(period, year) %>%
  summarize(
    below_days = sum(below_threshold, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

periodNumber of YearsAverage Below-Threshold Days per YearMinimum Days in a YearMaximum Days in a Year2000-20101149.501152011-20231343.70150


Lees Ferry averaged 49.5 below threshold days per year during 2000–2010 and 43.7 days per year during 2011–2023, indicating a slight decrease in low-flow days for the 2011-2023. However, the higher maximum value (150 days) after 2011 suggests that extreme low-flow years have become more frequent, even if average conditions have not worsened. This implies that while baseline conditions may not have shifted dramatically, the risk of extreme drought years still poses a significant challenge for reliably meeting Colorado River Compact delivery obligations.







basin_flow <- basin_flow %>%
  mutate(
    month = month(Date),
    water_year = if_else(
      month >= 10,
      year(Date) + 1,   
      year(Date)        
    )
  )

Check that worked

  group_by(site_no, water_year) %>%
  summarize(
    total_annual_discharge = sum(Flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    peak_daily_discharge   = max(Flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    days_below_threshold   = sum(below_threshold, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

lees_annual <- annual_metrics %>%
  filter(site_no == "09380000",
         water_year >= 1980,
         water_year <= 2023)


median_flow <- median(lees_annual$total_annual_discharge, na.rm = TRUE)


lees_annual <- lees_annual %>%
  mutate(
    flow_category = if_else(
      total_annual_discharge >= median_flow,
      "Above Median",
      "Below Median"
    )
  )


ggplot(lees_annual, aes(x = water_year, y = total_annual_discharge, fill = flow_category)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Above Median" = "blue", "Below Median" = "red")
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = median_flow,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Lees Ferry Annual Discharge by Water Year",
    subtitle = "Blue = above median, Red = below median",
    x = "Water Year",
    y = "Total Annual Discharge (cfs/days)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()









The record shows a structural shift toward lower annual discharge beginning in the early 2000s., with most years after 2000 fall below the long-term median compared to more frequent above-median years in the 1980s–1990s. One notable exception is 2011, which stands out as a very high-flow year due to an large snowpack and therefore strong spring runoff across the Colorado River Basin. This pattern indicates a drier baseline in recent decades, meaning Compact water accounting must account for fewer high-flow years and should adjust the baseline, to not over budget the system.








baseline_stats <- annual_metrics %>%
  filter(water_year >= 1980, water_year <= 2010) %>%
  group_by(site_no) %>%
  summarize(
    baseline_mean = mean(total_annual_discharge, na.rm = TRUE),
    baseline_sd   = sd(total_annual_discharge, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

annual_anomaly <- annual_anomaly %>%
  mutate(
    anomaly = (total_annual_discharge - baseline_mean) / baseline_sd
  )





annual_anomaly <- annual_anomaly %>%
  left_join(select(basin_flow, site_no, name) %>% distinct(), by = "site_no")


ggplot(annual_anomaly, 
       aes(x = water_year, 
           y = fct_reorder(str_wrap(name, width = 25), site_no), 
           fill = anomaly)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_distiller(
    palette = "RdBu",
    direction = 1,        # blue = wet, red = dry
    limits = c(-3, 3),    # optional but recommended
    oob = scales::squish, # clamps extreme values
    name = "Standardized\nAnomaly"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Standardized Streamflow Anomalies by Gauge and Water Year",
    subtitle = "Baseline: 1980–2010 (standard deviations from mean)",
    x = "Water Year",
    y = "Gauge"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8)
  )

Warning in mean.default(sort(x, partial = half + 0L:1L)[half + 0L:1L]):
argument is not numeric or logical: returning NA









The post-2000 drought signal appears consistently across nearly all gauges, with widespread negative anomalies indicating that dry conditions are basin-wide. A clear basin-wide dry year is 2002, driven by severe drought and low snow pack associated with La Niña conditions, while a basin-wide wet year is 2011, caused by a large snowpack linked to a La Niña enhanced winter. The synchronization of anomalies across gauges shows that the Colorado River Basin often responds as a connected system to climate events, meaning impacts to water availability and Compact obligations should be evaluated across the entire basin.







annual_geo <- annual_metrics %>%
  left_join(
    site_meta %>% select(site_no, dec_lat_va, dec_long_va),
    by = "site_no"
  )

Check

nrow(annual_geo) == nrow(annual_metrics)

flow_center <- annual_geo %>%
  group_by(water_year) %>%
  summarize(
    weighted_lat = sum(dec_lat_va * total_annual_discharge, na.rm = TRUE) / 
      sum(total_annual_discharge, na.rm = TRUE),
    weighted_lon = sum(dec_long_va * total_annual_discharge, na.rm = TRUE) / 
      sum(total_annual_discharge, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

west_map <- states_map %>%
  filter(region %in% c(
    "washington", "oregon", "california", "idaho", "nevada",
    "utah", "arizona", "montana", "wyoming", "colorado",
    "new mexico"

# Map with flow-weighted center through time
p_map <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = west_map,
    aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group),
    fill = "grey90",
    color = "white",
    linewidth = 0.2
  ) +
  geom_path(
    data = flow_center %>% arrange(water_year),
    aes(x = weighted_lon, y = weighted_lat, color = water_year),
    linewidth = 0.8,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = flow_center,
    aes(x = weighted_lon, y = weighted_lat, color = water_year),
    size = 2,
    alpha = 0.3
  ) +
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "blue",
    high = "red",
    name = "Water Year"
  ) +
  coord_fixed(xlim = c(-125, -102), ylim = c(30, 50)) +
  labs(
    title = "Flow-Weighted Center of Basin Streamflow",
    subtitle = "Annual center shifts through time",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

# Weighted latitude through time with linear trend
p_lat <- ggplot(flow_center, aes(x = water_year, y = weighted_lat)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, color = "black") +
  geom_point(aes(color = water_year), size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "blue",
    high = "red",
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Weighted Mean Latitude Through Time",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = linear trend",
    x = "Water Year",
    y = "Weighted Mean Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

p_map + p_lat

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









The two years where the weighted center shifts sharply northward are 1995 and 2023, both of which correspond to high snowpack years driven by strong winter storm activity (1995 from a series of Pacific storm tracks and 2023 from an intense atmospheric river–dominated winter). These years both have unusually large snow accumulation in the northern headwaters, which dominates total basin flow and pulls the flow-weighted center north. The long-term upward trend in weighted latitude suggests that northern portions of the basin are becoming important contributors to total flow, likely due to declining snowpack and increased aridity in southern tributaries. This matters for water managers because it indicates increasing reliance on northern water sources to meet downstream Compact obligations, reducing resilience if those headwater regions experience future drought.







basin_flow <- basin_flow %>%
  mutate(
    month = month(Date),
    season = case_when(
      month %in% 4:6   ~ "Snowmelt",
      month %in% 7:9   ~ "Monsoon",
      month %in% 10:12 ~ "Low-flow",
      month %in% 1:3   ~ "Winter"
    ),
    season = factor(
      season,
      levels = c("Snowmelt", "Monsoon", "Low-flow", "Winter")
    )
  )


table(basin_flow$season)


Snowmelt  Monsoon Low-flow   Winter 
   27299    27541    27600    26985 






seasonal_metrics <- basin_flow %>%
  group_by(site_no, name, water_year, season, drain_area_va) %>%
  summarize(
    total_seasonal_discharge = sum(Flow, na.rm = TRUE),
    normalized_runoff = total_seasonal_discharge / first(drain_area_va),
    .groups = "drop"
  )





peak_timing <- basin_flow %>%
  filter(!is.na(flow_7day)) %>%
  group_by(site_no, water_year) %>%
  slice_max(flow_7day, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    peak_doy = yday(Date)
  )





lees_peak <- peak_timing %>%
  filter(site_no == "09380000",
         water_year >= 1980,
         water_year <= 2023)

ggplot(lees_peak, aes(x = water_year, y = peak_doy)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Lees Ferry Peak 7-Day Mean Flow Timing by Water Year",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = linear trend",
    x = "Water Year",
    y = "Peak Day of Year"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









The trend shows a slight shift toward later peak timing, roughly 50–60 days later over the full record, though the data are highly scattered. The trend is weak and partially buried in noise, with large year-to-year variability that makes the signal difficult to distinguish clearly. If peak timing were instead two weeks earlier, reservoir managers would need to release or store water sooner in the spring, reducing flexibility and increasing the risk of missing peak runoff or spilling water before it can be captured for downstream use.






Lees Annual

lees_annual <- annual_metrics %>%
  filter(site_no == "09380000") %>%
  select(water_year, total_annual_discharge) %>%
  rename(lees_flow = total_annual_discharge)

Glenwood Springs

glenwood_annual <- annual_metrics %>%
  filter(site_no == "09085000") %>%
  select(water_year, total_annual_discharge) %>%
  rename(glenwood_flow = total_annual_discharge)

Join Lees and Glenwood

model_df <- lees_annual %>%
  left_join(glenwood_annual, by = "water_year")

Find Dominate Season at Lees

lees_season <- seasonal_metrics %>%
  filter(site_no == "09380000") %>%
  group_by(water_year) %>%
  slice_max(total_seasonal_discharge, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(water_year, season) %>%
  rename(dominant_season = season)

Join Dominant Season

model_df <- model_df %>%
  left_join(lees_season, by = "water_year")

Log Transform Flows

model_df <- model_df %>%
  mutate(
    log_lees = log(lees_flow),
    log_glenwood = log(glenwood_flow)
  )

Check

nrow(model_df)

Fit interaction model

The model explains a large portion of the variability in Lees Ferry flow (R² = 0.855) and is highly statistically significant (p < 0.001). The coefficient on log_glenwood (1.80) indicates a super-linear relationship: a 1% increase in flow at Glenwood Springs is associated with about a 1.8% increase at Lees Ferry, reflecting downstream amplification as additional tributaries contribute flow. The significant negative interaction terms for Monsoon, Low-flow, and Winter show that this upstream–downstream relationship is weaker outside of the Snowmelt season. This suggests that spring snowmelt provides a strong, basin-wide signal linking headwaters to downstream flow, while other seasons are more influenced by localized processes and contribute less predictability.






Add fitted predictions and residuals

glimpse(aug)

  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs. Actual Log Flow at Lees Ferry",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = perfect prediction",
    x = "Predicted log(Lees Flow)",
    y = "Actual log(Lees Flow)",
    color = "Season"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

  mutate(abs_resid = abs(.resid)) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_resid)) %>%
  select(water_year, dominant_season, log_lees, .fitted, .resid) %>%
  slice(1)

1       1983 Monsoon             16.0    15.4  0.566


The predicted vs. actual plot shows that most observations fall close to the 1:1 line, indicating good model performance. However, 1983 Monsoon stands out as a clear outlier, where the model substantially underpredicts Lees Ferry flow, suggesting that the model struggles to capture extreme hydrologic conditions, likely associated with unusual climate dynamics in that year.





ggplot(aug, aes(x = .resid)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "lightblue", 
                 color = "white" ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Histogram of Model Residuals",
    x = "Residuals",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw()








The clear outlier of the model is 1983, where the model substantially underpredicts flow at Lees Ferry. This year corresponds to a wet El Niño-driven winter, which produced unusually large basin-wide runoff that is not fully captured by Glenwood Springs alone. The residuals are roughly centered around zero but have a right-skewed tail, indicating occasional large underpredictions in extreme high-flow years. Two predictors that would improve the model are SWE, which directly controls spring runoff, and a climate index such as ENSO (El Niño/La Niña), which influences precipitation patterns and helps capture extreme wet or dry years that a single gauge cannot fully represent.

title: Exploring Streamflow Across the Colorado River Basin
text:
Compare runoff intensity across sites within different drainage areas by normalize by expressing flow per unit area: cfs per square mile.

Create a normalized flow column

basin_flow <- basin_flow %>%
  mutate(flow_per_sqmi = Flow / drain_area_va)

Filter to date and create a ranking table

flow_rank <- basin_flow %>%
  filter(Date == my.date) %>%
  mutate(
    raw_rank = min_rank(desc(Flow)),
    normalized_rank = min_rank(desc(flow_per_sqmi))
  ) %>%
  arrange(raw_rank) %>% 
   transmute(
    'Gauge Name' = name,
    'Raw Discharge (cfs)' = round(Flow, 1),
    'Drainage Area (mi²)' = round(drain_area_va, 1),
    'Normalized Flow (cfs/mi²)' = round(flow_per_sqmi, 3),
    'Raw Rank' = raw_rank,
    'Normalized Rank' = normalized_rank
  )

flow_rank_ft <- flextable(flow_rank) %>%
  set_caption("Raw and normalized discharge rankings for gauges on the selected dates") %>%
  autofit()

flow_rank_ft

Gauge NameRaw Discharge (cfs)Drainage Area (mi²)Normalized Flow (cfs/mi²)Raw RankNormalized RankLittle Colorado R near Cameron6,950141,6000.04915Colorado R at Lees Ferry6,570111,8000.05924Colorado R near CO-UT border3,47017,8490.19432San Juan R near Bluff68023,0000.03046Colorado R at Glenwood Springs5331,4530.36751White R near Watson3294,0200.08263Colorado R below Hoover Dam173,300


The table above shows that raw and normalized rankings differ substantially. The most dramatic change is for Colorado River at Glenwood Springs, which changes from 5th in raw discharge to 1st when normalized, indicating a much higher flow per unit area despite a smaller total flow.
Per-area normalization matters in the Colorado Basin because watersheds vary in size- large rivers naturally carry more total water, but smaller basins can be much more hydrologically “intense” when flow is scaled by drainage area, making comparisons more meaningful.
Raw discharge would be used for analyses such as water supply where total volume matters, while normalized discharge is better for comparing runoff efficiency across basins of different sizes.

The record shows a structural shift toward lower annual discharge beginning in the early 2000s., with most years after 2000 fall below the long-term median compared to more frequent above-median years in the 1980s–1990s. One notable exception is 2011, which stands out as a very high-flow year due to an large snowpack and therefore strong spring runoff across the Colorado River Basin. This pattern indicates a drier baseline in recent decades, meaning Compact water accounting must account for fewer high-flow years and should adjust the baseline, to not over budget the system.

The post-2000 drought signal appears consistently across nearly all gauges, with widespread negative anomalies indicating that dry conditions are basin-wide. A clear basin-wide dry year is 2002, driven by severe drought and low snow pack associated with La Niña conditions, while a basin-wide wet year is 2011, caused by a large snowpack linked to a La Niña enhanced winter. The synchronization of anomalies across gauges shows that the Colorado River Basin often responds as a connected system to climate events, meaning impacts to water availability and Compact obligations should be evaluated across the entire basin.

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









The two years where the weighted center shifts sharply northward are 1995 and 2023, both of which correspond to high snowpack years driven by strong winter storm activity (1995 from a series of Pacific storm tracks and 2023 from an intense atmospheric river–dominated winter). These years both have unusually large snow accumulation in the northern headwaters, which dominates total basin flow and pulls the flow-weighted center north. The long-term upward trend in weighted latitude suggests that northern portions of the basin are becoming important contributors to total flow, likely due to declining snowpack and increased aridity in southern tributaries. This matters for water managers because it indicates increasing reliance on northern water sources to meet downstream Compact obligations, reducing resilience if those headwater regions experience future drought.

The trend shows a slight shift toward later peak timing, roughly 50–60 days later over the full record, though the data are highly scattered. The trend is weak and partially buried in noise, with large year-to-year variability that makes the signal difficult to distinguish clearly. If peak timing were instead two weeks earlier, reservoir managers would need to release or store water sooner in the spring, reducing flexibility and increasing the risk of missing peak runoff or spilling water before it can be captured for downstream use.

The model explains a large portion of the variability in Lees Ferry flow (R² = 0.855) and is highly statistically significant (p < 0.001). The coefficient on log_glenwood (1.80) indicates a super-linear relationship: a 1% increase in flow at Glenwood Springs is associated with about a 1.8% increase at Lees Ferry, reflecting downstream amplification as additional tributaries contribute flow. The significant negative interaction terms for Monsoon, Low-flow, and Winter show that this upstream–downstream relationship is weaker outside of the Snowmelt season. This suggests that spring snowmelt provides a strong, basin-wide signal linking headwaters to downstream flow, while other seasons are more influenced by localized processes and contribute less predictability.

The predicted vs. actual plot shows that most observations fall close to the 1:1 line, indicating good model performance. ggplot(aug, aes(x = .resid)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 fill = "lightblue", 
                 color = "white" ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Histogram of Model Residuals",
    x = "Residuals",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw()








The clear outlier of the model is 1983, where the model substantially underpredicts flow at Lees Ferry. This year corresponds to a wet El Niño-driven winter, which produced unusually large basin-wide runoff that is not fully captured by Glenwood Springs alone. The residuals are roughly centered around zero but have a right-skewed tail, indicating occasional large underpredictions in extreme high-flow years. Two predictors that would improve the model are SWE, which directly controls spring runoff, and a climate index such as ENSO (El Niño/La Niña), which influences precipitation patterns and helps capture extreme wet or dry years that a single gauge cannot fully represent.

title: Lab 2: Distances and Projections
text:
Question 1: Building a Spatial Dataset

1.1 Define a Projection

eqdc <- '+proj=eqdc +lat_0=40 +lon_0=-96 +lat_1=20 +lat_2=60 +x_0=0 +y_0=0 +datum=NAD83 +units=m +no_defs'



1.2 CONUS State Boundaries

states <- tigris::states(cb = TRUE, progress_bar = FALSE) |>
  filter(!(STUSPS %in% c("AK", "HI", "DC", "PR", "VI", "GU", "MP", "AS"))) |>
  st_transform(eqdc)

Retrieving data for the year 2024



nrow(states) 

[1] 48




1.3 North American Country Boundaries1

countries <- rnaturalearth::ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") |>
  filter(admin %in% c("United States of America", "Canada", "Mexico")) |>
  st_transform(eqdc)



1.4 US City Locations
Load Data

cities_raw <- readr::read_csv("uscities.csv")

Rows: 31257 Columns: 17
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (9): city, city_ascii, state_id, state_name, county_fips, county_name, s...

lgl (2): military, incorporated

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.



glimpse(cities_raw)

Rows: 31,257
Columns: 17
$ city         <chr> "New York", "Los Angeles", "Chicago", "Miami", "Houston",…

Convert into SF object

cities <- cities_raw |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lng", "lat"), crs = 4326) |>
  st_transform(eqdc)

Filter to CONUS

conus_boundary <- st_union(states)

cities <- st_filter(cities, conus_boundary)

Check

st_crs(states) == st_crs(countries)

[1] TRUE

title: Lab 2: Distances and Projections
Lab 2: Distances and Projections
text:
Question 3: Mapping Distance Patterns

3.1 Reference Map

big_cities <- cities |>
  slice_max(population, n = 10)


ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = countries, fill = "grey85", color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = big_cities, color = "red", size = 2) +
  ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
    data = big_cities,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3
  ) +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Reference Map of North America and Major US Cities",
    subtitle = "Top 10 US cities by population",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )










3.2 Distance to National Border

far_border_ <- cities |>
  slice_max(dist_to_border, n = 5)


ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(color = dist_to_border), size = 0.4, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_sf(data = far_border_, color = "red", size = 2) +
  ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
    data = far_border_,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Distance to national border (km)") +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Distance of US Cities to the National Border",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )








The cities farthest from the national border are clustered in the middle of the country, which makes sense because CONUS is widest in its middle, so cities there are farthest from the coastlines and international borders. In water resources, distance to a national border could matter for transboundary watershed management near shared boundaries.


3.3 Distance to Nearest State Border

far_state <- cities |>
  slice_max(dist_to_state, n = 5)


ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(color = dist_to_state), size = 0.4, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_sf(data = far_state, color = "red", size = 2) +
  ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
    data = far_state,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Distance to state border (km)") +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Distance of US Cities to the Nearest State Border",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )








This pattern differs from 3.2 because it reflects distance from state lines, not just the outside border of the country. Larger states with bigger interiors, such as Texas, will have cities farther from a state border. A hydrologist could use this measure when thinking about interstate water governance, since cities far from state lines may be less influenced by border issues.


3.4 Equidistance Zone: Mexico and Canada

cities <- cities |>
  mutate(diff_mex_can = abs(dist_to_mexico - dist_to_canada))


equidistant_top <- cities |>
  filter(diff_mex_can <= 100) |>
  slice_max(population, n = 5)

Warning: Using one column matrices in `filter()` was deprecated in dplyr 1.1.0.
ℹ Please use one dimensional logical vectors instead.



ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(color = diff_mex_can), size = 0.4, alpha = 0.5) +
  gghighlight::gghighlight(diff_mex_can <= 100, use_direct_label = FALSE) +
  geom_sf(data = equidistant_top, color = "red", size = 2) +
  ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
    data = equidistant_top,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Mexico - Canada (km)") +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Cities Approximately Equidistant from Mexico and Canada",
    subtitle = "Highlighted where distance difference is within ±100 km",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )

Warning: Could not calculate the predicate for layer 1; ignored









The equidistant zone is a band across the United States, varying roughly where cities are in the middle of northern and southern international borders. It is not too surprising that this runs through the middle of the country, but is interesting how far south it appears in the East. This suggests that cities in that corridor may sit between Canadian and Mexican climate influences, rather than being clearly dominated by one or the other.

title: Lab 2: Distances and Projections
section: Question 4: Real-World Application — The 100-Mile Border Zone
text:
Question 4: Real-World Application — The 100-Mile Border Zone

4.1 Quantify the Zone
Get total population

total_pop <- cities |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  summarise(total_pop = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  pull(total_pop)

Filter cities within 160 km

zone_cities <- cities |>
  filter(dist_to_border <= 160)

Summarize

zone_summary <- zone_cities |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  summarise(
    n_cities = n(),
    zone_population = sum(population, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    percent_population = (zone_population / total_pop) * 100
  )


flextable(zone_summary)

n_citieszone_populationpercent_population13,363258,047,45465.24422


Approximately 65.24% of the US city population falls within 100 miles of the border, which is similar to the ACLU’s estimate of two thirds. The percentage is higher than many people expect as the US has large coastlines and many major cities are located near these boundaries. In water resources, similar jurisdictional boundaries arise in coastal zone management, where proximity to a boundary influences permitting and regulatory requirements.


4.2 Map the Zone

top10_zone <- zone_cities |>
  slice_max(population, n = 10)


ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(color = dist_to_border), size = 0.4, alpha = 0.4) +
  gghighlight::gghighlight(dist_to_border <= 160) +
  geom_sf(data = top10_zone, color = "black", size = 2) +
  ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
    data = top10_zone,
    aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
    stat = "sf_coordinates",
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_gradient(
    low = "orange",
    high = "darkred",
    name = "Distance to border (km)"
  ) +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Cities Within 100 Miles of the US Border",
    subtitle = "Highlighted cities fall within 160 km of the national boundary",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )

Warning: Could not calculate the predicate for layer 1; ignored











4.3 Most Populous City per State in the Zone

state_top_zone <- cities |>
  filter(dist_to_border <= 160) |>
  group_by(state_name) |>
  slice_max(population, n = 1) |>
  ungroup()


n_states <- n_distinct(state_top_zone$state_name)
n_states

[1] 35



ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = states, fill = NA, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = cities, color = "grey80", size = 0.3, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_sf(data = zone_cities, aes(color = dist_to_border), size = 0.5) +
  geom_sf(data = state_top_zone, color = "black", size = 2) +
 ggrepel::geom_label_repel(
  data = state_top_zone,
  aes(geometry = geometry, label = city),
  stat = "sf_coordinates",
  size = 3,
  max.overlaps = Inf
) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "orange", high = "darkred") +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Most Populous City per State Within 100-Mile Border Zone",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )








35 states have at least one city within the 100-mile border zone. States without cities in the zone tend to be located in the interior of the country. This highlights how jurisdictional boundaries based on distance can create challenges for environmental monitoring, particularly for water systems that span multiple states or cross national borders.

title: Lab 2: Distances and Projections
section: Question 5: Interactive Leaflet Exercise: Build an interactive map
text:
Question 5: Interactive Leaflet Exercise: Build an interactive map
Transform to leaflet projection

states_leaf <- st_transform(states, 4326)
cities_leaf <- st_transform(cities, 4326)

Create subsets

equidistant_cities <- cities_leaf |>
  filter(abs(dist_to_mexico - dist_to_canada) <= 100)

border_zone_cities <- cities_leaf |>
  filter(dist_to_border <= 160)

Color cities by distance to national border

pal_border <- colorNumeric(
  palette = "viridis",
  domain = cities_leaf$dist_to_border
)


Interactive Map of Cities Distance

cities_leaf <- cities_leaf |>
  mutate(
    popup = paste0(
      "<b>", city, ", ", state_id, "</b><br/>",
      "Population: ", format(population, big.mark = ","), "<br/>",
      "Distance to national border: ", round(dist_to_border, 1), " km<br/>",
      "Distance to nearest state border: ", round(dist_to_state, 1), " km<br/>",
      "Distance to Mexico: ", round(dist_to_mexico, 1), " km<br/>",
      "Distance to Canada: ", round(dist_to_canada, 1), " km"
    )
  )

equidistant_cities <- equidistant_cities |>
  mutate(
    popup = paste0(
      "<b>", city, ", ", state_id, "</b><br/>",
      "Population: ", format(population, big.mark = ","), "<br/>",
      "Distance to Mexico: ", round(dist_to_mexico, 1), " km<br/>",
      "Distance to Canada: ", round(dist_to_canada, 1), " km<br/>",
      "Difference: ", round(abs(dist_to_mexico - dist_to_canada), 1), " km"
    )
  )

border_zone_cities <- border_zone_cities |>
  mutate(
    popup = paste0(
      "<b>", city, ", ", state_id, "</b><br/>",
      "Population: ", format(population, big.mark = ","), "<br/>",
      "Distance to national border: ", round(dist_to_border, 1), " km"
    )
  )

leaflet() |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron, group = "CartoDB Positron") |>
  addProviderTiles(providers$OpenStreetMap, group = "OpenStreetMap") |>
  
  # state boundaries
  addPolygons(
    data = states_leaf,
    color = "black",
    weight = 1,
    fill = FALSE,
    group = "State Boundaries"
  ) |>
  
  # cities by distance
  addCircleMarkers(
    data = cities_leaf,
    radius = 2,
    stroke = FALSE,
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    color = ~pal_border(dist_to_border),
    popup = ~popup,
    group = "All Cities: Distance to National Border"
  ) |>
  
  # equidistance zone
  addCircleMarkers(
    data = equidistant_cities,
    radius = 2,
    stroke = TRUE,
    weight = 1,
    color = "blue",
    fillColor = "cyan",
    fillOpacity = 0.8,
    popup = ~popup,
    group = "Equidistance Zone (±100 km)"
  ) |>
  
  # 100-mile border zone
  addCircleMarkers(
    data = border_zone_cities,
    radius = 2,
    stroke = TRUE,
    weight = 1,
    color = "red",
    fillColor = "orange",
    fillOpacity = 0.8,
    popup = ~popup,
    group = "100-Mile Border Zone"
  ) |>
  
  addLegend(
    position = "bottomright",
    pal = pal_border,
    values = cities_leaf$dist_to_border,
    title = "Distance to National Border (km)",
    opacity = 1
  ) |>
  
  addLayersControl(
    baseGroups = c("CartoDB Positron", "OpenStreetMap"),
    overlayGroups = c(
      "State Boundaries",
      "All Cities: Distance to National Border",
      "Equidistance Zone (±100 km)",
      "100-Mile Border Zone"
    ),
    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)
  )





Distance calculations were tranformed to North America Equidistant Conic projection as it preserves distance more appropriately across CONUS. To display the data in Leaflet, all spatial layers were transformed from to WGS84, which is the coordinate system required for leaflet mapping. To keep the map responsive, I used simple, small circle markers and separated the equidistance and 100-mile border zones into seperate overlay layers.

title: Lab 3: MAUP in Practice — Tessellations and Point-in-Polygon
text:
Question 1: Conus

Step 1.1: Get Conus

counties <- AOI::aoi_get(state = "conus", county = "all") %>%
  st_transform(5070)


st_crs(counties)

Coordinate Reference System:
  User input: EPSG:5070 
  wkt:
PROJCRS["NAD83 / Conus Albers",
    BASEGEOGCRS["NAD83",
        DATUM["North American Datum 1983",
            ELLIPSOID["GRS 1980",6378137,298.257222101,
                LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]]],
        PRIMEM["Greenwich",0,
            ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]],
        ID["EPSG",4269]],
    CONVERSION["Conus Albers",
        METHOD["Albers Equal Area",
            ID["EPSG",9822]],
        PARAMETER["Latitude of false origin",23,
            ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433],
            ID["EPSG",8821]],
        PARAMETER["Longitude of false origin",-96,
            ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433],
            ID["EPSG",8822]],
        PARAMETER["Latitude of 1st standard parallel",29.5,
            ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433],
            ID["EPSG",8823]],
        PARAMETER["Latitude of 2nd standard parallel",45.5,
            ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433],
            ID["EPSG",8824]],
        PARAMETER["Easting at false origin",0,
            LENGTHUNIT["metre",1],
            ID["EPSG",8826]],
        PARAMETER["Northing at false origin",0,
            LENGTHUNIT["metre",1],
            ID["EPSG",8827]]],
    CS[Cartesian,2],
        AXIS["easting (X)",east,
            ORDER[1],
            LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]],
        AXIS["northing (Y)",north,
            ORDER[2],
            LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]],
    USAGE[
        SCOPE["Data analysis and small scale data presentation for contiguous lower 48 states."],
        AREA["United States (USA) - CONUS onshore - Alabama; Arizona; Arkansas; California; Colorado; Connecticut; Delaware; Florida; Georgia; Idaho; Illinois; Indiana; Iowa; Kansas; Kentucky; Louisiana; Maine; Maryland; Massachusetts; Michigan; Minnesota; Mississippi; Missouri; Montana; Nebraska; Nevada; New Hampshire; New Jersey; New Mexico; New York; North Carolina; North Dakota; Ohio; Oklahoma; Oregon; Pennsylvania; Rhode Island; South Carolina; South Dakota; Tennessee; Texas; Utah; Vermont; Virginia; Washington; West Virginia; Wisconsin; Wyoming."],
        BBOX[24.41,-124.79,49.38,-66.91]],
    ID["EPSG",5070]]

nrow(counties)

[1] 3108




Step 1.2 Generate Centroids and Combine

county_centroids <- st_centroid(counties)

county_centroids_multi <- county_centroids %>% 
  st_geometry() %>% 
  st_combine()


st_geometry_type(county_centroids_multi)

[1] MULTIPOINT
18 Levels: GEOMETRY POINT LINESTRING POLYGON MULTIPOINT ... TRIANGLE




Step 1.3: Create four tessellated surfaces and add individual IDs
Voronoi Tesselation

voronoi <- county_centroids_multi %>% 
  st_sfc(crs = 5070) %>% 
  st_voronoi() %>% 
  st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>% 
  st_as_sf() %>% 
  mutate(id = 1:n()) %>% 
  st_cast()

Triangulated Tesselation

delaunay <- county_centroids_multi %>% 
  st_sfc(crs = 5070) %>% 
  st_triangulate() %>% 
  st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>% 
  st_as_sf() %>% 
  mutate(id = 1:n()) %>% 
  st_cast()

Gridded Coverage

square_grid <- st_make_grid(counties, n = 70, square = TRUE) %>% 
  st_as_sf() %>% 
  mutate(id = 1:n()) %>% 
  st_cast()

Hexagonal Coverage

hex_grid <- st_make_grid(counties, n = 70, square = FALSE) %>% 
  st_as_sf() %>% 
  mutate(id = 1:n()) %>% 
  st_cast()

Check

nrow(voronoi)

[1] 3108

nrow(delaunay)

[1] 6198

nrow(square_grid)

[1] 4900

nrow(hex_grid)

[1] 3718




Step 1.4: Create single CONUS boundary and crop tessellations

conus_union <- counties %>% 
  summarise() %>% 
  st_cast()

Crop tessellations

voronoi_crop <- st_intersection(voronoi, conus_union)

delaunay_crop <- st_intersection(delaunay, conus_union)

Filter Grids

square_crop <- st_filter(square_grid, conus_union)

hex_crop <- st_filter(hex_grid, conus_union)



Step 1.5 Simplify boundary using Visvalingam Algorithm

boundary_original <- counties %>%
  summarise() %>%
  st_cast()

Simplify using Visvalingam

boundary_simplified <- boundary_original %>%
  rmapshaper::ms_simplify(keep = 0.02, keep_shapes = TRUE)

Validation Check

# Check: Is the simplified boundary valid?
st_is_valid(boundary_simplified)

[1] TRUE

# Check: Does it still contain all of CONUS?
st_bbox(boundary_simplified)

      xmin       ymin       xmax       ymax 
-2360167.5   259157.7  2256608.6  3177425.2 

# Check: Visual comparison
plot(boundary_original, main = "Original")







plot(boundary_simplified, main = "Simplified")








Compare points

original_pts <- mapview::npts(boundary_original)
simplified_pts <- mapview::npts(boundary_simplified)

original_pts

[1] 11292

simplified_pts

[1] 229

original_pts - simplified_pts

[1] 11063


The original CONUS boundary contained 11,292 vertices, while the simplified boundary contained only 229 vertices, resulting in a reduction of 11,063 vertices. This represents a decrease in complexity and increases computational efficiency due to the decrease in geometric comparisons required during clipping. This does have a precision trade-off, which is important along coastlines and borders, but it’s okay for this use since it is not being used for analysis.
Now crop

voronoi_crop <- st_intersection(voronoi, boundary_simplified)
delaunay_crop <- st_intersection(delaunay, boundary_simplified)

square_crop <- st_filter(square_grid, boundary_simplified)
hex_crop <- st_filter(hex_grid, boundary_simplified)



Step 1.6: Function for plotting

plot_surface <- function(arg1, arg2) {
  ggplot(data = arg1) +
    geom_sf(fill = "white", color = "navy", linewidth = 0.2) +
    theme_void() +
    labs(
      title = arg2,
      caption = paste("Number of features:", nrow(arg1))
    )
}



Step 1.7: Create Plots

p1 <- plot_surface(counties, "CONUS Counties")
p1









p2 <- plot_surface(voronoi_crop, "Voronoi Tessellation")
p2









p3 <- plot_surface(delaunay_crop, "Delaunay Triangulation")
p3









p4 <- plot_surface(square_crop, "Square Grid Coverage")
p4









p5 <- plot_surface(hex_crop, "Hexagonal Grid Coverage")
p5

section: Question 2: Functions to summarize tessellated surfaces
text:
Question 2: Functions to summarize tessellated surfaces

Step 2.1 Function to take an sf object and create a data frame

sum_tess <- function(arg1, arg2) {
  
  area_km2 <- arg1 %>%
    st_area() %>%
    set_units(km^2) %>%
    drop_units()
  
  data.frame(
    surface = arg2,
    n_features = nrow(arg1),
    mean_area_km2 = mean(area_km2),
    sd_area_km2 = sd(area_km2),
    total_area_km2 = sum(area_km2)
  )
}



Step 2.2 Summarize each surface

sum_tess(counties, "Counties")

1 Voronoi       3107      2603.935    2914.885        8090426

sum_tess(delaunay_crop, "Delaunay")

   surface n_features mean_area_km2 sd_area_km2 total_area_km2
1 Delaunay       6198      1290.379    1599.368        7997771

sum_tess(square_crop, "Square Grid")

sum_tess(hex_crop, "Hex Grid")

   surface n_features mean_area_km2  sd_area_km2 total_area_km2
1 Hex Grid       2297      3781.179 1.812003e-11        8685368




Step 2.3 Bind them into a table

tess_summary <- bind_rows(
  sum_tess(counties, "Counties"),
  sum_tess(voronoi_crop, "Voronoi"),
  sum_tess(delaunay_crop, "Delaunay"),
  sum_tess(square_crop, "Square Grid"),
  sum_tess(hex_crop, "Hex Grid")
)



Step 2.4 Print as a nice table

  mutate(
    mean_area_km2 = round(mean_area_km2, 2),
    sd_area_km2 = round(sd_area_km2, 2),

  ) %>%

The tessellations show clear differences in feature count and area, which directly affect point-in-polygon results. Counties and Voronoi polygons have similar mean areas, but counties show higher variability due to irregular boundaries, making them more susceptible to MAUP. Voronoi reduces some bias by using centroid-based proximity but still reflects uneven spatial distribution of counties.
Delaunay has the highest number of features andthe smallest mean area, allowing for finer spatial resolution and better detection of local patterns, but at increased computational cost. In contrast, square and hex grids have uniform areas, which improves consistency in analysis and reduces MAUP effects related to area variability. However, their coarser resolution (especially hex) may smooth over local detail.
Delaunay is most computationally expensive due to the high feature count, while hex grids are most efficient. Overall, finer tessellations capture more spatial detail but increase computational demand, while regular grids improve efficiency at the cost of resolution.

title: Lab 3: MAUP in Practice — Tessellations and Point-in-Polygon
section: Question 3: Dam Distribution Analysis and Tesselation Selection
text:
Question 3: Dam Distribution Analysis and Tesselation Selection
Add potential extra packages

library(janitor)
library(viridis)
library(patchwork)


Step 3.1 Read and Clean Dam Data

# Prefer a local NID CSV named `nation.csv`; otherwise download to that filename
nid_url <- "https://nid.sec.usace.army.mil/api/nation/csv"
dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)
nid_dest <- "data/nation.csv"

if (file.exists(nid_dest)) {
  message("Using local NID file: ", nid_dest)
} else {
  tryCatch({
    message("Downloading NID CSV to: ", nid_dest)
    download.file(nid_url, nid_dest, quiet = FALSE, mode = "wb")
  }, error = function(e) {
    message("NID download failed: ", e$message)
  })
}

# read the CSV (FIXED to avoid parsing issues)
if (!file.exists(nid_dest)) stop("NID CSV not found at ", nid_dest)

dams <- readr::read_csv(
  nid_dest,
  skip = 1,
  show_col_types = FALSE,
  col_types = cols(.default = col_character())  
) %>% 
  janitor::clean_names()

# convert coordinates to numeric
dams <- dams %>%
  mutate(
    latitude = as.numeric(latitude),
    longitude = as.numeric(longitude)
  )

# CONUS boundary
usa <- AOI::aoi_get(state = "conus") %>% 
  st_union() %>% 
  st_transform(5070)

# convert to sf and filter
dams2 <- dams %>% 
  filter(!is.na(latitude), !is.na(longitude)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 4326) %>% 
  st_transform(5070) %>% 
  st_filter(usa)



Step 3.2 Point in Polygon Function

pip_count <- function(arg1, arg2, arg3) {
  
  id_sym <- rlang::sym(arg3)
  
  counts <- st_join(arg2, arg1, left = TRUE) %>%
    st_drop_geometry() %>%
    count(!!id_sym, name = "n")
  
  out <- arg2 %>%
    left_join(counts, by = arg3) %>%
    mutate(n = replace_na(n, 0))
  
  return(out)
}



Step 3.3 Apply point in polygon counts to five surfaces

counties_dams  <- pip_count(dams2, counties, "fip_code")
voronoi_dams   <- pip_count(dams2, voronoi_crop, "id")
delaunay_dams  <- pip_count(dams2, delaunay_crop, "id")
square_dams    <- pip_count(dams2, square_crop, "id")
hex_dams       <- pip_count(dams2, hex_crop, "id")



Step 3.4 Plotting function for dam courts

plot_dams <- function(arg1, arg2) {
  ggplot(data = arg1) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = n), color = NA) +
    scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "viridis", na.value = "white") +
    theme_void() +
    labs(
      title = arg2,
      caption = paste("Number of dams:", sum(arg1$n, na.rm = TRUE))
    )
}



Step 3.5 Plot!

p1 <- plot_dams(counties_dams, "Counties")
p1









p2 <- plot_dams(voronoi_dams, "Voronoi")
p2









p3 <- plot_dams(delaunay_dams, "Delaunay")
p3









p4 <- plot_dams(square_dams, "Square Grid")
p4









p5 <- plot_dams(hex_dams, "Hex Grid")
p5










Step 3.6 Policy Analysis
a. Side by side comparison of dam clustering under different tessellations.

(p1 | p2 | p3) / (p4 | p5)








c. Which single tessellation would you recommend to a dam operations manager seeking to understand geographic clustering? Justify your choice by addressing: - Does the tessellation align with natural or management decision-making units (e.g., river basins, states, hydrologic regions)? - Does it reveal meaningful patterns without over-fragmenting the landscape or creating artificial boundaries? - How does MAUP influence your recommendation—are other tessellations equally valid, just for different questions? - How did simplification affect your choice? Would a coarser or finer simplification (different keep percentage) change your recommendation? - What does “best supports management decisions” mean concretely in your context—does it matter more that hot spots are obvious, or that boundaries align with jurisdictions?
As seen in the image above, several regions show clear differences based on tessellation choice. In the Southeast, Delaunay and Voronoi have localized hotspots, while the square and hex grids have broader areas of moderate density. This occurs because Delaunay has 6,198 tiles, the smallest mean area of 1290 km squared, and captures fine-scale variation. Meanwhile, grids have fewer tiles, uniform area, and aggregate counts over larger, consistent units. In the Midwest, Voronoi reveals clustered patterns with centroid spacing, while counties show more irregular patterns due to high area variability (sd 3443 km squared). In contrast, grid-based tessellations reduce this variability (sd = 0), producing more uniform and less detailed results. In the Western U.S., counties and Voronoi have clustering along river systems and developed corridors, while hex and square grids generalize these into larger regions, masking finer structure. This is due to geometry and tile size as Voronoi responds to centroid spacing, while grids have fixed spatial units. As shown in Q2, grids have larger mean areas (square at 2761 km squared, hex at 3781 km squared), which smooths local clustering. These differences illustrate MAUP, where changing the size and shape of spatial units alters the observed distribution of dams.
For a dam operations manager, I would recommend the hexagonal grid tessellation. It provides a good balance between detail and clarity. Counties have very uneven sizes, which can make patterns harder to understand, while square grids can introduce artificial, blocky patterns due to their alignment. On the other hand Delaunay creates many small tiles, which adds a lot of detail but can make the map harder to read. The hex grid avoids these issues by using consistent, evenly sized areas which makes it easier to see and compare regional clustering patterns. While it does not perfectly match natural boundaries like river basins, it still provides a clear and consistent way to visualize where dams are concentrated.
MAUP influences this decision, since different tessellations can show different patterns from the same data. No single method is “correct” as each highlights different aspects of the distribution. Delaunay is useful for identifying very fine-scale clustering, while counties may be more useful when decisions need to align with administrative boundaries. The simplification step (keep ≈ 0.02) only affects the outer boundary used for clipping and improves computational efficiency, so it does not change the main patterns seen in the tessellations. Overall, the hex grid is the best choice because it clearly shows meaningful hotspots without adding unnecessary complexity, making it easier for decision-makers to interpret and use.

title: Lab 3: MAUP in Practice — Tessellations and Point-in-Polygon
text:
Question 4:

Step 4.1 Filter and Count Dams
Select purposes that represent major categories of dams tied to energy production (H), risk management (C), water use (I), and public use (R), to compare across different management priorities.

hydro_dams <- dams2 %>%
  filter(grepl("H", purposes))

flood_dams <- dams2 %>%
  filter(grepl("C", purposes))

irrigation_dams <- dams2 %>%
  filter(grepl("I", purposes))

recreation_dams <- dams2 %>%
  filter(grepl("R", purposes))

Count each subset across the cropped hexagonal tessellation

hydro_hex <- pip_count(hydro_dams, hex_crop, "id")
flood_hex <- pip_count(flood_dams, hex_crop, "id")
irrigation_hex <- pip_count(irrigation_dams, hex_crop, "id")
recreation_hex <- pip_count(recreation_dams, hex_crop, "id")



Step 4.2 Highlight Hotspots

hydro_thresh <- mean(hydro_hex$n) + sd(hydro_hex$n)
flood_thresh <- mean(flood_hex$n) + sd(flood_hex$n)
irrigation_thresh <- mean(irrigation_hex$n) + sd(irrigation_hex$n)
recreation_thresh <- mean(recreation_hex$n) + sd(recreation_hex$n)


p_hydro <- plot_dams(hydro_hex, "Hydroelectric Dams") +
  gghighlight(n > hydro_thresh)

p_flood <- plot_dams(flood_hex, "Flood Control Dams") +
  gghighlight(n > flood_thresh)

p_irrigation <- plot_dams(irrigation_hex, "Irrigation Dams") +
  gghighlight(n > irrigation_thresh)

p_recreation <- plot_dams(recreation_hex, "Recreation Dams") +
  gghighlight(n > recreation_thresh)


p_hydro









p_flood









p_irrigation









p_recreation










Step 4.3 Geographic Distribution of Dams
The geographic distribution of dams generally makes sense and reflects known physical and human factors. Hydroelectric dams are concentrated in mountainous and high-flow areas such as the Pacific Northwest. Flood control dams are clustered in the central U.S., especially around the Mississippi River basin, where flood risk is higher. Irrigation dams are most common in the western United States, particularly in arid regions like California where water storage is critical for agriculture. Recreation dams are more widely distributed but show strong clustering near populated areas, where demand for recreation is high. These patterns align closely with major river systems, climate, and topography. Areas with dense river networks and higher precipitation have more flood control dams, while arid regions rely heavily on irrigation dam infrastructure. Mountainous regions support hydroelectric dams due to steeper gradients and higher energy potential. This confirms that dam placement is strongly tied to both natural hydrology and human water needs.
The hexagonal tessellation helps reveal these patterns clearly by using consistent spatial units, but it also smooths some local variation. Smaller, more detailed tessellations (like Delaunay) might show finer clustering along specific rivers, while larger or more uniform grids may generalize patterns across regions. This means that tessellation choice can influence how “clustered” dams appear, even though the underlying distribution is the same.
I would expect irrigation and hydroelectric dams to show different LISA hotspot patterns compared to other dams. Irrigation dams will likely cluster in the arid West, while hydroelectric dams will cluster in mountainous regions, leading to more specialized and region-specific hotspots.

title: Lab 3: MAUP in Practice — Tessellations and Point-in-Polygon
section: Question 5: Spatial Correlation of Dam Attributes: Introduction to Spatial Regression
text:
Question 5: Spatial Correlation of Dam Attributes: Introduction to Spatial Regression

Step 5.1 Aggregate dam attributes by tile
Convert data to numeric

dams2 <- dams %>%
  mutate(
    latitude = as.numeric(latitude),
    longitude = as.numeric(longitude),
    year_completed = as.numeric(year_completed),
    surface_area_acres = as.numeric(surface_area_acres)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(latitude), !is.na(longitude)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 4326) %>%
  st_transform(5070) %>%
  st_filter(usa)

Aggregate

hex_summary <- st_join(hex_crop, dams2, left = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(id) %>%
  summarise(
    n_dams = sum(!is.na(nid_id)),
    mean_year_completed = mean(year_completed, na.rm = TRUE),
    var_year_completed = var(year_completed, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_surface_area = mean(surface_area_acres, na.rm = TRUE),
    var_surface_area = var(surface_area_acres, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    n_dams = replace_na(n_dams, 0),
    mean_year_completed = ifelse(is.nan(mean_year_completed), NA, mean_year_completed),
    var_year_completed = ifelse(is.nan(var_year_completed), NA, var_year_completed),
    mean_surface_area = ifelse(is.nan(mean_surface_area), NA, mean_surface_area),
    var_surface_area = ifelse(is.nan(var_surface_area), NA, var_surface_area),
    mean_age = 2026 - mean_year_completed
  )



Step 5.2 Neighbor Correlation: Local Spatial Validation
Build Neighbors and Spatial Weights

hex_nb <- poly2nb(hex_summary, queen = TRUE)
hex_listw <- nb2listw(hex_nb, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
W_std <- listw2mat(hex_listw)

Build Attribute Vectors

count_vec <- hex_summary$n_dams
age_vec   <- hex_summary$mean_age
size_vec  <- hex_summary$mean_surface_area

Handle NAs

age_mean_global  <- mean(age_vec, na.rm = TRUE)
size_mean_global <- mean(size_vec, na.rm = TRUE)

age_imp  <- ifelse(is.na(age_vec), age_mean_global, age_vec)
size_imp <- ifelse(is.na(size_vec), size_mean_global, size_vec)
count_imp <- count_vec

Compute Spatial Lag

lag_count <- as.numeric(W_std %*% count_imp)
lag_age   <- as.numeric(W_std %*% age_imp)
lag_size  <- as.numeric(W_std %*% size_imp)

Correlations

cor_count <- cor(count_vec, lag_count, use = "complete.obs")

cor_age <- cor(
  age_vec[!is.na(age_vec)],
  lag_age[!is.na(age_vec)]
)

cor_size <- cor(
  size_vec[!is.na(size_vec)],
  lag_size[!is.na(size_vec)]
)

Compact Summary Table

strength_label <- function(x) {
  case_when(
    abs(x) < 0.20 ~ "Weak",
    abs(x) < 0.50 ~ "Moderate",
    TRUE ~ "Strong"
  )
}

lag_summary <- tibble(
  attribute = c("Dam count", "Mean dam age", "Mean surface area"),
  correlation_with_spatial_lag = c(cor_count, cor_age, cor_size)
) %>%
  mutate(
    correlation_with_spatial_lag = round(correlation_with_spatial_lag, 3),
    strength = strength_label(correlation_with_spatial_lag)
  )

lag_summary

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  attribute         correlation_with_spatial_lag strength
  <chr>                                    <dbl> <chr>   
1 Dam count                                0.837 Strong  
2 Mean dam age                             0.645 Strong  
3 Mean surface area                        0.001 Weak    




Step 5.3 Moran’s I scatterplot
Standardize Attribute and Lag

z_count <- scale(count_vec, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]
lag_z_count <- scale(lag_count, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)[, 1]

Assign Quadrants

moran_df <- tibble(
  z = z_count,
  lag_z = lag_z_count
) %>%
  mutate(
    quadrant = case_when(
      z > 0 & lag_z > 0 ~ "HH",
      z < 0 & lag_z < 0 ~ "LL",
      z > 0 & lag_z < 0 ~ "HL",
      z < 0 & lag_z > 0 ~ "LH",
      TRUE ~ "Axis"
    )
  )


moran_df %>%
  count(quadrant)

# A tibble: 4 × 2
  quadrant     n
  <chr>    <int>
1 HH         523
2 HL         113
3 LH         169
4 LL        1492


Moran Scatterplot

ggplot(moran_df, aes(x = z, y = lag_z)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Moran Scatterplot for Dam Count",
    x = "Standardized Dam Count",
    y = "Standardized Spatial Lag of Dam Count"
  )










Step 5.4 Global Interpretation of Spatial Patterns
Spatial Clustering Patterns:
The spatial lag correlations indicate that several dam attributes exhibit strong spatial clustering across neighboring hexagonal tiles. Dam count shows the strongest spatial dependence with a correlation of 0.837. This indicates that areas with many dams are typically surrounded by other high-density dam regions. Mean dam age also shows strong clustering, with a correlation of 0.645. This suggests that dams within neighboring tiles were often constructed during similar time periods. In contrast, mean surface area has a near-zero correlation (0.001), indicating no meaningful spatial structure for dam size.
These results suggest that both dam density and age are spatially organized at a regional scale, while dam size is variable and doesn’t follow a consistent pattern. The strong clustering in dam count likely reflects underlying hydrologic and human factors, such as river network density and regional water demand. Similarly, the clustering of dam age suggests that construction occurred in regionally coordinated periods, such as development projects. The lack of spatial correlation in surface area implies that dam size is independent rather than regional patterns.
Moran Scatterplot Interpretation:
The Moran scatterplot for dam count supports these findings by showing a clear positive relationship between standardized dam count and spatial lag. The fitted regression line has a strong positive slope, and many points cluster along the line, indicating positive spatial autocorrelation. The quadrant counts further reinforce this pattern: there are 523 high-high (HH) and 1492 low-low (LL) observations, compared to fewer high-low (113) and low-high (169) outliers.
This distribution indicates that tiles with high dam density tend to be surrounded by other high density tiles, while low density tiles are typically adjacent to other low density areas. The relatively smaller number of HL and LH points suggests that abrupt changes between neighboring tiles are less common, and that dam distribution is spatially coherent rather than random.
Spatial Structure Implications:
Overall, these results demonstrate strong spatial organization in dam infrastructure across the United States. Neighboring tiles are similar in terms of dam count and dam age, indicating that spatial dependence is present and that the assumption of independent observations in standard regression would be violated. This supports the need for spatial modeling approaches, such as spatial lag, for analyzing dam related variables.
The observed spatial patterns are consistent with known hydrologic and management factors. Regions with extensive river systems and higher water demand, such as the Southeast, tend to have higher dam densities, creating clusters of high values. Similarly, historical dam construction efforts often occurred in coordinated regional phases, leading to clustering in dam age. In contrast, dam size is influenced by site-specific engineering, topography, and storage needs, which mayexplain the lack of correlation. These findings align with patterns observed in Question 4, where dam purposes were also geographically clustered according to regional needs such as irrigation, flood control, and hydroelectric power. The results suggest that dam infrastructure is strongly shaped by both regional hydrology and human decision-making, leading to clear spatial dependence in key attributes.

title: Lab 3: MAUP in Practice — Tessellations and Point-in-Polygon
text:
Question 6: Lisa Mapping Hotspots

Step 6.1 Crop Tesselations to Consistent Boundary

hex_cropped <- st_filter(hex_grid, boundary_simplified)

county_units <- counties

hex_dams <- st_join(hex_cropped, dams2, left = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(id) %>%
  summarise(
    n = sum(!is.na(nid_id)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(n = replace_na(n, 0))

county_dams <- st_join(county_units, dams2, left = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(fip_code) %>%
  summarise(
    n = sum(!is.na(nid_id)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(n = replace_na(n, 0))



Step 6.2 Neighbors and Lags for Tessellations

make_lisa_objects <- function(sf_obj, value_col) {
  
  W <- st_touches(sf_obj, sparse = FALSE)
  diag(W) <- FALSE
  W_num <- W * 1
  
  rs <- rowSums(W_num)
  W_std <- W_num
  
  if (any(rs > 0)) {
    W_std[rs > 0, ] <- sweep(W_num[rs > 0, , drop = FALSE], 1, rs[rs > 0], "/")
  }
  if (any(rs == 0)) {
    W_std[rs == 0, ] <- 0
  }
  
  x <- sf_obj[[value_col]]
  lag_x <- as.numeric(W_std %*% x)
  
  z <- as.numeric(scale(x, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE))
  lag_z <- as.numeric(scale(lag_x, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE))
  
  list(
    W = W_num,
    W_std = W_std,
    row_sums = rowSums(W_std),
    isolated = sum(rowSums(W_std) == 0),
    x = x,
    lag_x = lag_x,
    z = z,
    lag_z = lag_z
  )
}


hex_objs <- make_lisa_objects(hex_dams, "n")
county_objs <- make_lisa_objects(county_dams, "n")

Step 6.3 Locality
Assign LISA Quadrants

assign_quadrants <- function(sf_obj, z, lag_z) {
  sf_obj %>%
    mutate(
      z = z,
      lag_z = lag_z,
      quadrant = case_when(
        z > 0 & lag_z > 0 ~ "HH",
        z < 0 & lag_z < 0 ~ "LL",
        z > 0 & lag_z < 0 ~ "HL",
        z < 0 & lag_z > 0 ~ "LH",
        TRUE ~ "Axis"
      )
    )
}


hex_lisa_map <- assign_quadrants(hex_dams, hex_objs$z, hex_objs$lag_z)
county_lisa_map <- assign_quadrants(county_dams, county_objs$z, county_objs$lag_z)

Moran Scatterplots

plot_moran <- function(sf_obj, title_text) {
  ggplot(sf_obj, aes(x = z, y = lag_z)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(
      title = title_text,
      x = "Standardized Dam Count",
      y = "Standardized Spatial Lag of Dam Count"
    )
}


p_hex_moran <- plot_moran(hex_lisa_map, "Moran Scatterplot: Hexagons")

p_hex_moran









p_county_moran <- plot_moran(county_lisa_map, "Moran Scatterplot: Counties")

p_county_moran








Lisa Geographic Maps

lisa_colors <- c(
  "HH" = "red",
  "LL" = "blue",
  "HL" = "yellow",
  "LH" = "cyan",
  "Axis" = "grey80"

    geom_sf(aes(fill = quadrant), color = NA) +
